title: Advanced Project
summary: Design and implement a multi-module application with concurrency, persistence, and testing

sections.title: Project Architecture: Event-Driven Design
sections.content: This project uses an event-driven architecture where components communicate through events, like a newsroom where reporters file stories and editors pick them up. An OrderPlaced event triggers inventory deduction. A PaymentReceived event triggers shipping. This decouples components — the order service doesn't need to know about shipping, it just publishes events. You'll implement this with a simple in-memory event bus and thread pools. This pattern is used in real production systems by Netflix, Uber, and Amazon because it scales horizontally: each component can run on separate servers connected by a message queue like Kafka or RabbitMQ.

sections.title: Event Bus with Concurrent Processing
sections.content: This shows the core event bus that dispatches events to multiple subscribers concurrently

sections.title: Bringing It All Together: Layers and Testing
sections.content: Your project will have three modules: core (domain models and events), persistence (database access with JDBC/HikariCP), and service (business logic and event processing). Each module is built, tested, and versioned independently. The persistence module depends on core, and service depends on both. You'll write JUnit 5 tests with Mockito for the service layer (mocking persistence), and integration tests using an H2 in-memory database for the persistence layer. This multi-module setup mirrors real enterprise projects at companies like Spotify and Twitter where teams own different modules and communicate through well-defined interfaces.

sections.title: Not separating integration tests from unit tests
sections.content: Most common mistake: mixing fast unit tests (milliseconds) with slow integration tests (seconds) in the same test run, making developers avoid running tests. Fix: use Maven's test vs failsafe plugin separation, or JUnit tags to distinguish categories. Run unit tests on every save, integration tests only before commit. Wrong: all tests in src/test/java with no categories. Correct: unit tests in src/test/java, integration tests in src/integration-test/java.

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: Design and implement a mini stock trading system with three modules. The order-service module validates trades (a buy order needs sufficient funds, a sell order needs sufficient shares). The market-data module publishes price changes as events. The reporting module listens for trade execution events and generates daily summaries. Use the EventBus pattern, JDBC for persistence (H2 in-memory), and a thread pool for concurrent order processing. Write at least 5 JUnit tests for the order validation logic and 2 integration tests for persistence.
hints: What events does the order-service publish when a trade executes?
How do you test concurrent order processing without actual threads?
What database schema captures accounts, holdings, and trade history?
